title: Why PARTE measures a thermal melting curve, not the ground truth of pairing

blocks.text: If you look only at the data table, PARTE is easily misread as a structure detector that "settles the pairing state of a base at a given temperature", as if it took a snapshot at each of 23, 30, 37, 55 and 75°C, each directly telling you who is paired and who is unpaired. That reading is wrong. PARTE (Parallel Analysis of RNA structures with Temperature Elevation, Wan et al.) is the temperature extension of PARS: at a series of temperatures (such as 23, 30, 37, 55 and 75°C), it uses structure-specific nucleases (the double-strand-specific RNase V1 and the single-strand-specific S1) to cleave the RNA, and then reads out, by deep sequencing, how the enzymatic cleavage at each site changes with temperature. That is to say, what PARTE reports first is the melting/thermal stability (melting profile) at each site, at which temperature a stretch of structure begins to destabilise and how its probability of being cleaved changes, rather than directly whether "this base is definitely paired" or "this base is definitely unpaired".

What PARTE actually reads is an entire melting curve, not the cleavage snapshot at any single temperature, so the multi-temperature readings must not be merged prematurely. A temperature-dependent enzymatic cleavage signal is not equivalent to a single pairing call. FoldBridge can preserve the raw multi-temperature cleavage signal and can display the experimental melting readout on its web pages; but as soon as anything enters default structure colouring, interpretive text or confidence, a more specific question must be asked first: can this number, in the enzymatic-cleavage chemistry and temperature context of PARTE, be interpreted as evidence of the thermal stability at that site? The raw V1/S1 reads must first pass the temperature/readout context before they can become a candidate for default colour or confidence.
The experimental chain: V1 enzymatic cleavage at multiple temperatures reads out the melting difference

blocks.legend: **Figure 1. The PARTE experimental schematic and the per-nucleotide melting of SCR1.** Wan et al. 2012 (PMID 22981864, DOI `10.1016/j.molcel.2012.08.008`). The illustration breaks PARTE down: (A) the differential melting of RNA secondary structure, read out by deep sequencing the fragments generated by the double-strand-specific RNase V1, where each aligned sequence provides double-stranded structural information for a base; the more reads aligned to a base, the more it has been cleaved by V1, and the more likely it is to be double-stranded. (B) The raw V1 sequencing reads of the first 150 nt of the SCR1 RNA at 23, 30, 37, 55 and 75°C, with the coloured bar at the bottom marking the regions that melt at different temperatures, the colour corresponding to the last temperature at which the structure remains stable. (C) The UV absorbance of full-length SCR1 at 260 nM and its first derivative with respect to temperature (20-95°C), with the single-nucleotide-resolution melting transitions obtained from PARTE marked in colour. (D) The secondary structure of SCR1 (Zwieb et al., 2005), where the grey dashed line indicates a tertiary interaction, and the melting transitions obtained from PARTE are marked with coloured dots.
body: What PARTE measures is the thermal melting curve (melting profile) of RNA structure, not the static ground truth of pairing at a given temperature. High V1 reads suggest that the site is double-stranded at that point and has not yet melted; a fall in reads with temperature suggests that this stretch of structure is beginning to destabilise and unwind. The core variable of the readout is "how enzyme accessibility changes with temperature", not a single-point paired/unpaired label.

For FoldBridge, this means that the values in the final table are not "structure labels" given directly by V1, but observations after multi-temperature cleavage, library preparation, reverse transcription, sequencing and normalisation. This observation is very useful, but what it reports is the per-position thermal stability, which must first be interpreted within the enzymatic-cleavage and temperature context of PARTE before structure can be discussed.
blocks.label: PARTE F1
blocks.legendText: A , Schematic of PARTE experiment. Differential melting of RNA secondary structures is read out by deep sequencing of RNA fragments generated by double-stranded specific RNase V1. Each aligned sequence provides double stranded structural information about a base (represented as a red or green square). A large number of reads aligned to a base indicates that the base was cleaved many times by RNase V1 and is hence more likely to be double-stranded. B , Raw RNase V1 sequencing reads for the first 150nt of the SCR1 RNA at 23, 30, 37, 55 and 75°C. Regions that melt at different temperatures were shown as colored bars at the bottom of the graph. The colored bar indicates the last temperature that the structure was found stable. C , UV absorbance of full length SCR1 RNA at 260nM, as well as first derivative of the UV absorbance over temperature, was obtained from 20-95°C . Nucleotide resolution melting transitions obtained by PARTE are indicated in color. D , RNA secondary structure of SCR1 ( Zwieb et al., 2005 ). A tertiary interaction is indicated as grey dotted lines. The melting transitions obtained from PARTE are indicated as colored dots.

blocks.text: The temperature-dependent distribution of enzymatic cleavage: the double-stranded signal falls overall at high temperature

blocks.legend: **Figure 2. The change of V1 cleavage with temperature and the per-base melting transition.** Wan et al. 2012 (PMID 22981864, DOI `10.1016/j.molcel.2012.08.008`). It is organised in three parts: (A) the Pearson correlation of V1 reads (log2) between biological replicates and between samples at different temperatures: biological replicates have the highest correlation (Pearson≈0.7), and the greater the temperature difference between two samples, the lower the correlation of their per-base V1 profiles, which reflects the difference in the amount of pairing at that base. (B) The distribution of V1 cleavage with temperature: counting bases that have at least two V1 reads at 23°C, as temperature rises the proportion of bases with high V1 reads falls overall, with more and more bases going from having V1 reads at 23°C to having low or no reads at high temperature, suggesting that the RNA structure melts at high temperature. (C) Using Stepminer (Sahoo et al., 2007) to identify the temperature transition point of each base, where each row indicates the temperature at which V1 cleavage jumps from low to high or from high to low, with two columns for each of the five temperatures as biological replicates; blue indicates more than two times below the mean normalised V1 reads, and yellow more than two times above the mean.
body: This figure puts an easily overlooked safeguard on the table: the PARTE signal is only meaningful when placed in temperature coordinates. The V1 reads of the same site at 23°C and 75°C should not be equal in the first place, and their difference is precisely the melting information itself. Flattening readings at different temperatures into a single "ground truth of pairing" amounts to throwing away the most central variable of PARTE.

Brought to FoldBridge, this is exactly why a raw observation must carry its temperature coordinate. A V1 count cannot be interpreted apart from the temperature at which it was acquired; the high/low reads of the same site mean different things at different temperatures. Treating a value without a temperature label directly as the ground truth of double-stranded pairing conflates experimental parameters with structural interpretation.
blocks.label: PARTE F2
blocks.legendText: A , Pearson correlation of log2 of RNase V1 reads between biological replicates and samples of different temperatures. Biological replicates typically have the highest Pearson correlation (Pearson= 0.7). The greater the difference in temperature, the lower the Pearson correlation between two samples in their RNase V1 profile per base, which reflects the amount of pairing at that base. B , Distribution of RNase V1 cleavages with temperature. Bases with at least two RNase V1 reads at 23°C were analyzed for the distribution of RNase V1 reads with increasing temperature. In general, the percentage of bases with high V1 reads decreases with increasing temperature as more bases change from having V1 reads at 23°C, to having low or no V1 reads at higher temperatures, indicating melting of RNA structures at higher temperatures. C , Temperature transition per base is identified using Stepminer program( Sahoo et al., 2007 ). Each row indicates the temperature at which RNase V1 cleavages transit from low to high or high to low reads. The two columns in each of the five temperatures are biological replicates. Blue indicates two fold decrease from the mean of normalized RNase V1 reads, and yellow indicates two fold increase from the mean of normalized RNase V1 reads.

blocks.text: Different RNA classes have different melting temperatures: mRNA melts more readily than ncRNA

blocks.legend: **Figure 3. The melting temperature (Tm) distribution of different RNA classes.** Wan et al. 2012 (PMID 22981864, DOI `10.1016/j.molcel.2012.08.008`). It groups, by RNA class, the bases for which a Tm can be reliably determined, including coding RNAs and non-coding RNAs in the yeast transcriptome, human lincRNAs (fragments of HOTAIR and HOTTIP) and ribozymes (the P4P6 and P9-9.2 domains of the Tetrahymena ribozyme). (A) As shown, mRNA begins to melt at a lower temperature than ncRNA, while structurally functional ncRNA has a higher melting temperature. (B) The estimated Tm of a gene is taken as the mean Tm of the bases belonging to that gene; ncRNA (red box) is significantly more thermostable than yeast mRNA (blue box).
body: This figure places the value of PARTE in the right position: when the melting signal is aggregated to the RNA class level, it can indeed distinguish the "more stably structured" ncRNA from the "more readily melting" mRNA. This is something single-temperature probing cannot do, because thermal stability is itself a cross-temperature quantity.

But this is precisely also where it is most easily misread. A high Tm in PARTE corresponds to this stretch of structure melting only at a higher temperature, not simply "more paired". A stretch of structure can be locally paired yet globally unstable and melt at a low temperature; it can also be additionally stabilised by tertiary interactions and the ionic environment and have a higher Tm. Pairing, tertiary structure and the thermodynamic environment all take part in the interpretation. For FoldBridge, translating the Tm of PARTE directly into "paired/unpaired here" skips the layer of thermal stability that is actually being measured.
blocks.label: PARTE F3
blocks.legendText: A , The bases with confidently called Tms are separated into different classes of RNAs, which include coding, non-coding RNAs in the yeast transcriptome, human lincRNAs (fragments of HOTAIR and HOTTIP) and ribozymes (P4P6 and P9-9.2 domains of the Tetrahymena ribozyme). As shown in the graphs, mRNAs melt at lower temperatures than ncRNAs that often have structural roles. B, The estimated Tm of a gene was calculated as the average of all the Tm of the bases that belong to that gene. ncRNAs (red box) are significantly more thermo-stable than yeast mRNAs (blue box).

blocks.text: The signal has functional meaning: melting features near translation start/stop sites

blocks.legend: **Figure 4. The melting features around translation start and stop sites.** Wan et al. 2012 (PMID 22981864, DOI `10.1016/j.molcel.2012.08.008`). It analyses mRNAs with a coding region of at least 200 bases, aligned by start and stop codons: (A) the normalised V1 reads, averaged over all such transcripts and aligned by the start and stop codons (brown dashed lines). (B) The relative abundance of bases of different Tm around the start (left) and stop (right) codons, with the total number of Tm at each position normalised to 1. (C) The mean Tm curves of the 5′UTR, coding region and 3′UTR: the −3 to +3 nt region upstream of the start codon has the lowest mean Tm, while the region 20 nt upstream and 10 nt downstream of the stop codon has the highest Tm; a null distribution is obtained by randomising the Tm positions along the gene body 100 times to compute P values, with the dashed lines indicating the p=0.01 significance level (red: 1% probability of observing the Tm or higher; blue: 1% probability of observing the Tm or lower), and the black line being the mean estimated Tm of all yeast genes at that position. (D) The mean GC content around the start (left) and stop (right) sites.
body: The significance of this figure is not that "PARTE can determine the structure of every base", but a reminder: per-position melting temperatures, aggregated, reveal function-related patterns, structure being looser (low Tm) near the translation start site to facilitate ribosome entry, and more stable (high Tm) near the stop site. These are downstream interpretations of the melting profile, not the ground truth of pairing of some base.

For FoldBridge, the conclusion is direct: the strength of PARTE is to characterise thermal stability along the temperature dimension and to link it to functional sites; the error occurs when an aggregated Tm or the V1 reads at a single temperature are stuffed into the same confidence channel as equivalent "pairing evidence". GC content, local sequence and tertiary structure all affect Tm, and folding all of these together into "paired/unpaired" makes the information of the temperature dimension disappear.
blocks.label: PARTE F4
blocks.legendText: A , Normalized V1 reads were averaged across all mRNA transcripts that have at least 200 bases in their coding region and aligned by their start and stop codons (dotted brown lines). B, The relative abundance of bases with different Tms around the start (left) and stop (left) codon. The total number of Tm values at each position is normalized to 1. C , Average Tm profiles across 5′UTR, coding region and 3′UTR. The -3 to +3nt region upstream of the start codon has the lowest average Tm, while the 20nt upstream and 10nt downstream of the stop codon has the highest Tm. The locations of the Tm values are randomized for 100 times along the gene body to obtain the null distributions of estimated Tms, which was used to calculate the P values. The dashed lines indicate the significance level of p =0.01 (red: 1% chance of obtaining the observed or higher estimated Tm; blue: 1% chance of obtaining the observed or lower estimated Tm). The black line indicates the estimated Tm averaged across all the yeast genes for that particular base that is surrounding the translation start site (left) and the translation stop site (right). D , The average GC content around the translation start site (left) and the translation stop site (right).

blocks.text: How FoldBridge should write about PARTE

The core rule for PARTE in FoldBridge is to treat the temperature-dependent enzymatic-cleavage signal as evidence of per-position thermal stability (melting/Tm), not as a pairing call. The raw V1/S1 reads are first of all provenance: they record how many times a site was cleaved at a certain temperature under a certain nuclease. Only when this set of multi-temperature signals can be interpreted within the enzymatic-cleavage chemistry context of PARTE (V1 reports double-stranded, S1 reports single-stranded, and what is read out is the change with temperature) and the readout/mapping context do they enter the default visualisation and confidence.

The key to this boundary is to separate thermostability from paired. Thermally stable is not equal to paired: tertiary interactions, ionic conditions and sequence composition all make the Tm of a stretch of structure deviate from its secondary structure state at a fixed temperature. A paired helix can melt at a low temperature, while a seemingly loose structure can be stabilised by tertiary interactions up to a high temperature. What PARTE measures is "at how high a temperature this stretch of structure destabilises", not "whether these bases are paired at this moment".

So FoldBridge needs the concept of a gate, rather than a rule code written out of thin air. The duty of this gate is: before sending a raw V1/S1 value into default structure colouring or confidence, first confirm that it carries the label of its acquisition temperature (the kind of temperature/readout context in Figure 2), confirm that it has been reliably mapped to structural coordinates, and confirm that it is interpreted as temperature-dependent thermal stability rather than single-point pairing. Only signal that passes the gate enters the default visual track and the confidence candidate set; what does not pass is retained as raw provenance, debug information or download-table fields.

This is not a weakening of PARTE. On the contrary, it respects its strengths. PARTE is very sensitive to the thermal stability of structure and can also link melting features to functional sites; the error occurs when this strength is flattened into "high V1 reads equal pairing". So for a non-specialist reader, the most apt way to put it is "PARTE turns the melting process of structure with temperature into a sequenceable thermal-stability clue". A more complete sentence should read: in the PARTE assay, this site shows a melting transition from double-stranded to single-stranded over a certain temperature range; if the temperature label, mapping, coverage and context all pass the checks, this melting signal can serve as support for structural interpretation. This sentence is a little wordier than "high reads equal pairing", but it is scientifically sounder and more suited to entering FoldBridge's long-term knowledge base.
There is no real FoldBridge accession here, so what follows is a clearly labelled illustrative scenario, used to show how a single PARTE profile separates into three different layers.

Imagine a PARTE profile flowing through the three layers of FoldBridge. The first layer is the raw observation: the multi-temperature counts of a stretch of RNA cleaved by RNase V1 at 23, 30, 37, 55 and 75°C, preserved together with the acquisition temperature, nuclease type and the provenance of library preparation and sequencing. This layer answers only "what is recorded in the raw data", does not draw conclusions for the reader, and does not merge readings at different temperatures prematurely.

The second layer is the visual signal: only after these counts already carry their temperature labels, have been reliably mapped to structural coordinates and are interpreted as per-position melting/thermal stability do they enter default structure colouring. The colour the reader sees on the page should answer "within the interpretive boundary of PARTE, what readable thermal-stability clue does this site have (at which temperature it begins to melt)", not "is this base paired". Sites whose temperature is unknown, or that have not yet been mapped to reliable coordinates, should not be mixed into this layer pretending to be the same kind of evidence.

The third layer is the confidence candidate: only melting signal that has passed the temperature context, mapping and context checks may enter confidence. Even from the same profile, the raw V1 reads, the visualisation colour and the confidence travel three different paths.

What this illustrative scenario wants to convey is system behaviour, not any one set of numbers: a PARTE profile simultaneously grows the raw multi-temperature observation, the visualisation signal and the confidence candidate, these three layers each having their own semantics, and none of which may compress readings at different temperatures into a single number prematurely. If an article kneads them together, the reader will either read "melts only at high temperature" directly as "the ground truth of pairing", or take the reading at a single temperature to represent the entire curve.

So the correct ending for a PARTE article is not "high V1 reads prove the base is paired", but: PARTE data can provide a powerful thermal-stability (melting profile) clue for RNA structure interpretation; in FoldBridge, such a clue becomes part of structural interpretation and confidence only after first being interpreted as melting information that changes with temperature (rather than single-point pairing) and passing the subsequent gates.
